session.user: list all capabilities available fo users to install
session.thinking: The user wants to see all capabilities available for users to install. Let me explore the codebase to find what capabilities are available.

tool_use.name: Agent
tool_use.param: FIRST check docs/learned/index.md for existing documentation on this topic. Read relevant ...[truncated 487 chars]...ook at the capabilities/ directory structure and any enum or registry of capability names.

session.tool_result:
Perfect! Now let me compile all the information I've gathered. Let me create a comprehensive summary:
## Summary: All Available Erk Capabilities
Based on my exploration of the erk codebase, here's the complete list of capabilities available for users to install. The capabilities are defined in `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/core/capabilities/registry.py` which is the single source of truth for all registered capabilities.
### How Capabilities Work
1. **Registration**: All capabilities are registered in `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/core/capabilities/registry.py` via the `_all_capabilities()` function 2. **Installation**: Users can install/uninstall capabilities via `erk init capability add <name>` and `erk init capability remove <name>` commands 3. **Discovery**: List all capabilities with `erk init capability list` 4. **Scopes**: Capabilities are either "project" (per-repository) or "user" (global) 5. **Required**: Some capabilities auto-install during `erk init` (marked as `required=True`)
### Complete Capabilities List
#### REQUIRED CAPABILITIES (auto-installed during erk init)
| Name | Type | Scope | Description | |------|------|-------|-------------| | `learned-docs` | Documentation System | Project | Autolearning documentation system for agent-discoverable docs | | `erk-impl-workflow` | Workflow | Project | GitHub Action for automated implementation | | `code-reviews-system` | GitHub Infrastructure | Project | GitHub Actions infrastructure for convention-based code reviews | | `erk-hooks` | Hooks/Settings | Project | Configure Claude Code hooks for session management |
**File locations:** - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/capabilities/learned_docs.py` - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/capabilities/workflows/erk_impl.py` - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/capabilities/code_reviews_system.py` - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/capabilities/hooks.py`
... [120 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
Here are all **29 capabilities** available for users to install via `erk init capability add <name>`:
### Required (auto-installed during `erk init`)
| Name | Description | |------|-------------| | `learned-docs` | Autolearning documentation system for agent-discoverable docs | | `erk-impl-workflow` | GitHub Action for automated implementation | | `code-reviews-system` | GitHub Actions infrastructure for convention-based code reviews | | `erk-hooks` | Configure Claude Code hooks for session management |
### Bundled Skills (12)
| Name | Description | |------|-------------| | `cli-push-down` | Moving mechanical computation from prompts to exec commands | | `dignified-python` | Python coding standards (LBYL, modern types, ABCs) | | `fake-driven-testing` | 5-layer test architecture with fakes | | `erk-diff-analysis` | Code diff analysis for commit messages | | `erk-exec` | Erk exec subcommand reference | | `erk-planning` | Plan management | | `objective` | Objective tracking and management | | `gh` | GitHub CLI integration | | `gt` | Graphite stacked PR management | | `dignified-code-simplifier` | Code simplification review | | `pr-operations` | Pull request operations | | `pr-feedback-classifier` | PR feedback classification |
### Code Reviews (3) — require `code-reviews-system`
| Name | Description | |------|-------------| | `review-tripwires` | Tripwires code review for detecting dangerous patterns | | `review-dignified-python` | Dignified Python style code review | | `review-dignified-code-simplifier` | Code simplification suggestions review |
### Workflows (4)
| Name | Description | |------|-------------| | `learn-workflow` | GitHub Action for learn documentation workflow | | `one-shot-workflow` | GitHub Action for one-shot workflow | | `pr-address-workflow` | GitHub Action for PR address workflow | | `pr-rebase-workflow` | GitHub Action for PR rebase workflow |
### Agent (1)
| Name | Description | |------|-------------| | `devrun-agent` | Safe execution agent for pytest/ty/ruff/make/gt |
### Reminders (4) — opt-in context injection
| Name | Description | |------|-------------| | `devrun-reminder` | Remind agent to use devrun for CI tool commands | | `dignified-python-reminder` | Remind agent to follow dignified-python standards | | `explore-docs-reminder` | Remind agent to include doc-first instructions in Explore prompts | | `tripwires-reminder` | Remind agent to check tripwires.md |
### Settings/Configuration (3)
| Name | Description | |------|-------------| | `erk-bash-permissions` | Allow `Bash(erk:*)` commands in Claude Code | | `ruff-format` | Auto-format Python files with ruff after Write/Edit | | `statusline` | Claude Code status line configuration (user-scoped) |
All capabilities are **project-scoped** except `statusline` which is **user-scoped**. Install with `erk init capability add <name>`, remove with `erk init capability remove <name>`, list with `erk init capability list`.
session.user:
┌─────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Name │ Description │ ├─────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ learn-workflow │ GitHub Action for learn documentation workflow │ ├─────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ one-shot-workflow │ GitHub Action for one-shot workflow │ ├─────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ pr-address-workflow │ GitHub Action for PR address workflow │ ├─────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ pr-rebase-workflow │ GitHub Action for PR rebase workflow │ └─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────
----
include these by default
----
│ pr-operations │ Pull request operations │ ├───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ pr-feedback-classifier │ PR feedback classification
│ erk-diff-analysis │ Code diff analysis for commit messages │ ├───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ erk-exec │ Erk exec subcommand reference
│ objective │ Objective tracking and management

also include these by default
│ erk-impl-workflow │ GitHub Action for automated implementation │
default

The user wants me to make several capabilities required (auto-installed by default). Let me identify which ones:
1. `learn-workflow` 2. `one-shot-workflow` 3. `pr-address-workflow` 4. `pr-rebase-workflow` 5. `pr-operations` 6. `pr-feedback-classifier` 7. `erk-diff-analysis` 8. `erk-exec` 9. `objective` 10. `erk-impl-workflow` (already required based on my earlier research, but user is confirming)

session.tool_result:
Perfect! Now I have most of the information. Let me create a comprehensive summary by compiling all the findings:
## Summary: `required` Field Locations for All Specified Capabilities
Based on my code analysis, here are the exact file paths and line numbers where `required` is set (or defaults) for each capability:
### 1. **learn-workflow** - **File**: `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/capabilities/workflows/learn.py` - **Class**: `LearnWorkflowCapability` - **Setting**: Lines 33-35 ```python @property def required(self) -> bool: return True ```
### 2. **one-shot-workflow** - **File**: `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/capabilities/workflows/one_shot.py` - **Class**: `OneShotWorkflowCapability` - **Setting**: Lines 33-35 ```python @property def required(self) -> bool: return True ```
### 3. **pr-address-workflow** - **File**: `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/capabilities/workflows/pr_address.py` - **Class**: `PrAddressWorkflowCapability` - **Setting**: Lines 33-35
... [79 lines omitted] ...
session.thinking:
So the workflows are already `required=True`. The bundled skills (pr-operations, pr-feedback-classifier, erk-diff-analysis, erk-exec, objective) default to `required=False` via the base class.
I need to make those 5 bundled skills required. Let me read the bundled.py file to see how best to do this.
Also, erk-impl-workflow is already required. The user mentioned it but it's already done.
Let me read the bundled.py file.
session.assistant: The 4 workflow capabilities and `erk-impl-workflow` are already `required=True`. I need to make the 5 bundled skills required. Let me read the bundled skills file.
session.tool_result:
1→"""Bundled skill capabilities — cached factory. 2→ 3→All simple skill capabilities (those that only need a name and description) 4→are registered here. The dict is created lazily via a cached function to 5→avoid module-import-time allocation. 6→ 7→Skills with custom install/uninstall logic (like learned-docs) are NOT here; 8→they have their own capability classes. 9→""" 10→ 11→from functools import cache 12→ 13→from erk.core.capabilities.codex_portable import codex_portable_skills 14→from erk.core.capabilities.skill_capability import SkillCapability 15→from erk_shared.context.types import AgentBackend 16→ 17→ 18→@cache 19→def bundled_skills() -> dict[str, str]: 20→ """Return the bundled skills dict. Cached to avoid re-creation.""" 21→ return { 22→ "cli-push-down": "Moving mechanical computation from prompts to exec commands", 23→ "dignified-python": "Python coding standards (LBYL, modern types, ABCs)", 24→ "fake-driven-testing": "5-layer test architecture with fakes", 25→ "erk-diff-analysis": "Code diff analysis for commit messages", 26→ "erk-exec": "Erk exec subcommand reference", 27→ "erk-planning": "Plan management", 28→ "objective": "Objective tracking and management", 29→ "gh": "GitHub CLI integration", 30→ "gt": "Graphite stacked PR management",
... [35 lines omitted] ...

1→"""Base class for skill-based capabilities. 2→ 3→SkillCapability wraps the existing artifact sync system to install Claude skills 4→to external repositories via the capability system. 5→""" 6→ 7→import shutil 8→from abc import abstractmethod 9→from pathlib import Path 10→ 11→from erk.artifacts.paths import get_bundled_claude_dir 12→from erk.artifacts.state import add_installed_capability, remove_installed_capability 13→from erk.core.capabilities.base import ( 14→ Capability, 15→ CapabilityArtifact, 16→ CapabilityResult, 17→ CapabilityScope, 18→ ManagedArtifact, 19→ backend_agent_dir, 20→) 21→from erk_shared.context.types import AgentBackend 22→ 23→ 24→class SkillCapability(Capability): 25→ """Base class for capabilities that install a single skill. 26→ 27→ Subclasses only need to implement skill_name and description. 28→ The base class handles artifact tracking and installation via artifact sync. 29→ """ 30→
... [113 lines omitted] ...
session.thinking:
Now I understand the structure. The `BundledSkillCapability` inherits from `SkillCapability` which inherits from `Capability`, and `required` defaults to `False` in the base `Capability` class.
To make specific bundled skills required, the cleanest approach is to: 1. Add a `_REQUIRED_BUNDLED_SKILLS` set in bundled.py 2. Override `required` property in `BundledSkillCapability` to check membership
Let me also check which workflows are already required vs not.
Actually, the user said: 1. The 4 workflow capabilities (learn-workflow, one-shot-workflow, pr-address-workflow, pr-rebase-workflow) - "include these by default" → already required=True per the explore agent
2. pr-operations, pr-feedback-classifier, erk-diff-analysis, erk-exec, objective - "also include these by default" → need to become required 3. erk-impl-workflow - "default" → already required=True
So I need to make 5 bundled skills required. Let me write the plan.
session.assistant: Now I have the full picture. Let me write the plan.

PreToolUse:ExitPlanMode hook error: [command -v erk >/dev/null 2>&1 || exit 0; ERK_HOOK_ID=exit-plan-mode-hook erk exec exit-plan-mode-hook]: DISPLAY PLAN: Before asking the question below, read the plan file and display its contents to the user with proper markdown formatting: /Users/schrockn/.claude/plans/delegated-weaving-babbage.md
PLAN SAVE PROMPT
A plan exists for this session but has not been saved.
Use AskUserQuestion to ask the user: question: "📋 Make additional capabilities required by default Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)\n\nWhat would you like to do with this plan?" header: "br:master"
IMPORTANT: Present options in this exact order: 1. "Create a plan PR" (Recommended) - Create a draft PR with the plan and stop. Does NOT proceed to implementation. 2. "Skip PR and implement here" - Skip creating a PR, implement directly in current worktree (for small PR iterations that don't need PR tracking). 3. "View/Edit the plan" - Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.
⚠️ WARNING: Currently on 'master'. We strongly discourage editing directly on the trunk branch. Consider saving the plan and implementing in a dedicated worktree instead.
If user chooses 'Create a plan PR': 1. Run /erk:plan-save 2. STOP - Do NOT call ExitPlanMode. The plan-save command handles everything. Stay in plan mode and let the user exit manually if desired.
If user chooses 'Skip PR and implement here': 1. Create implement-now marker (skip PR creation): erk exec marker create --session-id dde102f8-f26b-464a-a801-edb171c55897 \ exit-plan-mode-hook.implement-now 2. Call ExitPlanMode 3. After exiting plan mode, implement the changes directly
... [7 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
Here's the plan:
## Make additional capabilities required by default
### Context
Several bundled skill capabilities that are broadly useful should auto-install during `erk init` instead of requiring manual `erk init capability add`. The 4 workflow capabilities and `erk-impl-workflow` are already `required=True`. Five bundled skills need to become required as well.
### Changes
**File: `src/erk/capabilities/skills/bundled.py`**
1. Add a module-level `_REQUIRED_BUNDLED_SKILLS` frozenset containing the 5 skill names: - `erk-diff-analysis` - `erk-exec` - `objective` - `pr-operations` - `pr-feedback-classifier`
2. Override the `required` property on `BundledSkillCapability` to check membership
No other files need changes — the registry already discovers `required` via the property.
### Verification
- Run `erk init capability list` and confirm the 5 skills show as required - Run tests: `pytest tests/ -k capability`

tool_use.name: AskUserQuestion
tool_use.param: [{'question': '📋 Make additional capabilities required by default\nCurrent context: (wt:er...[truncated 432 chars]...ion': 'Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

I don't want them visible to the user as capabilities. they are just part of the system. the required capabilities should be largely invisible
session.thinking:
The user wants these capabilities to not be visible to users at all - they should just be silently installed as part of the system without appearing in `erk init capability list`. Let me understand the current architecture better to figure out the right approach.
Let me look at how capabilities are listed and how the "required" ones are handled vs optional ones.

1→"""Capability registry - hardcoded list of all capabilities.""" 2→ 3→from functools import cache 4→ 5→from erk.capabilities.agents.devrun import DevrunAgentCapability 6→from erk.capabilities.code_reviews_system import CodeReviewsSystemCapability 7→from erk.capabilities.erk_bash_permissions import ErkBashPermissionsCapability 8→from erk.capabilities.hooks import HooksCapability 9→from erk.capabilities.learned_docs import LearnedDocsCapability 10→from erk.capabilities.reminders.devrun import DevrunReminderCapability 11→from erk.capabilities.reminders.dignified_python import DignifiedPythonReminderCapability 12→from erk.capabilities.reminders.explore_docs import ExploreDocsReminderCapability 13→from erk.capabilities.reminders.tripwires import TripwiresReminderCapability 14→from erk.capabilities.reviews.dignified_code_simplifier import ( 15→ DignifiedCodeSimplifierReviewDefCapability, 16→) 17→from erk.capabilities.reviews.dignified_python import DignifiedPythonReviewDefCapability 18→from erk.capabilities.reviews.tripwires import TripwiresReviewDefCapability 19→from erk.capabilities.ruff_format import RuffFormatCapability 20→from erk.capabilities.skills.bundled import create_bundled_skill_capabilities 21→from erk.capabilities.statusline import StatuslineCapability 22→from erk.capabilities.workflows.erk_impl import ErkImplWorkflowCapability 23→from erk.capabilities.workflows.learn import LearnWorkflowCapability 24→from erk.capabilities.workflows.one_shot import OneShotWorkflowCapability 25→from erk.capabilities.workflows.pr_address import PrAddressWorkflowCapability 26→from erk.capabilities.workflows.pr_rebase import PrRebaseWorkflowCapability 27→from erk.core.capabilities.base import Capability 28→from erk_shared.context.types import AgentBackend 29→ 30→
... [103 lines omitted] ...

1→"""List capabilities and their installation status.""" 2→ 3→from pathlib import Path 4→ 5→import click 6→ 7→from erk.cli.commands.init.capability.backend_utils import resolve_backend 8→from erk.core.capabilities.base import Capability, CapabilityArtifact 9→from erk.core.capabilities.registry import get_capability, list_capabilities 10→from erk.core.context import ErkContext 11→from erk.core.repo_discovery import NoRepoSentinel, discover_repo_or_sentinel 12→from erk_shared.context.types import AgentBackend 13→from erk_shared.output.output import user_output 14→ 15→ 16→@click.command("list") 17→@click.argument("name", required=False) 18→@click.pass_obj 19→def list_cmd(ctx: ErkContext, name: str | None) -> None: 20→ """List capabilities and their installation status. 21→ 22→ Without NAME: shows all capabilities with installed status. 23→ With NAME: shows detailed status for that specific capability. 24→ 25→ Project-level capabilities require being in a git repository. 26→ User-level capabilities can be checked from anywhere. 27→ """ 28→ # Lazy repo discovery - only done if needed 29→ erk_root = ctx.erk_installation.root() 30→ repo_or_sentinel = discover_repo_or_sentinel(ctx.cwd, erk_root, ctx.git)
... [107 lines omitted] ...

session.thinking:
Now I understand. The user wants these capabilities to be invisible - not showing up in `erk init capability list`. Currently `list_capabilities()` returns ALL capabilities. I need to add a concept of "visible" vs "internal" capabilities, where required/system capabilities are hidden from the user-facing list.
Let me look at the base Capability class to see the full property set.
session.tool_result:
1→"""Base classes and types for the capability system. 2→ 3→Capabilities are optional features that can be installed via `erk init capability add <name>`. 4→Each capability knows how to detect if it's installed and how to install itself. 5→""" 6→ 7→from abc import ABC, abstractmethod 8→from dataclasses import dataclass 9→from pathlib import Path 10→from typing import Literal 11→ 12→from erk_shared.context.types import AgentBackend 13→ 14→# Type alias for capability scope 15→CapabilityScope = Literal["project", "user"] 16→ 17→# Artifact types that capabilities can declare they manage 18→ManagedArtifactType = Literal[ 19→ "skill", "command", "agent", "workflow", "action", "hook", "prompt", "review" 20→] 21→ 22→ 23→@dataclass(frozen=True) 24→class ManagedArtifact: 25→ """Describes an artifact managed by a capability for artifact detection. 26→ 27→ This bridges capability declarations with artifact detection/health checks. 28→ Used by the registry to determine which artifacts are erk-managed. 29→ """ 30→
... [165 lines omitted] ...
61→ raise ValueError(f"Unknown agent backend: {backend!r}")
session.assistant: Now let me check how the `list_capabilities` function is consumed — specifically in the `add` and `remove` commands and `erk init`.
session.tool_result: src/erk/core/capabilities/registry.py:76:def list_capabilities() -> list[Capability]: src/erk/core/capabilities/registry.py:85:def list_required_capabilities() -> list[Capability]: src/erk/core/capabilities/registry.py:94:def list_capabilities_for_backend(*, backend: AgentBackend) -> list[Capability]: src/erk/cli/commands/init/main.py:17:from erk.core.capabilities.registry import list_capabilities, list_required_capabilities src/erk/cli/commands/init/main.py:561: for cap in list_required_capabilities(): src/erk/cli/commands/init/main.py:640: for cap in list_required_capabilities(): src/erk/cli/commands/init/main.py:682: all_caps = list_capabilities() src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/remove_cmd.py:8:from erk.core.capabilities.registry import get_capability, list_capabilities src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/remove_cmd.py:44: for c in list_capabilities(): src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/add_cmd.py:8:from erk.core.capabilities.registry import get_capability, list_capabilities src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/add_cmd.py:45: for c in list_capabilities(): src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/list_cmd.py:9:from erk.core.capabilities.registry import get_capability, list_capabilities src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/list_cmd.py:51: for c in list_capabilities(): src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/list_cmd.py:114: caps = list_capabilities() src/erk/artifacts/sync.py:760: from erk.core.capabilities.registry import list_capabilities src/erk/artifacts/sync.py:763: for cap in list_capabilities():

550→ 551→ # Sync artifacts with force=True to ensure all files are updated 552→ config = create_artifact_sync_config(repo_root, backend=backend) 553→ sync_result = sync_artifacts(repo_root, True, config=config) 554→ if sync_result.success: 555→ user_output(click.style(" ✓ ", fg="green") + sync_result.message) 556→ else: 557→ warn_msg = f"Artifact sync failed: {sync_result.message}" 558→ user_output(click.style(" ⚠ ", fg="yellow") + warn_msg) 559→ 560→ # Re-install required capabilities (e.g., hooks) 561→ for cap in list_required_capabilities(): 562→ if backend not in cap.supported_backends: 563→ continue 564→ check_repo_root = repo_root if cap.scope == "project" else None 565→ result = cap.install(check_repo_root, backend=backend) 566→ if result.success: 567→ user_output(click.style(" ✓ ", fg="green") + result.message) 568→ else: 569→ warn_msg = f"{cap.name} install failed: {result.message}" 570→ user_output(click.style(" ⚠ ", fg="yellow") + warn_msg) 571→ 572→ # Sync gitignore entries 573→ interactive = not no_interactive 574→ missing = find_missing_gitignore_entries(repo_root) 575→ if missing: 576→ if interactive: 577→ click.echo(f" Missing .gitignore entries: {', '.join(missing)}") 578→ if click.confirm(" Add missing entries to .gitignore?", default=True): 579→ add_missing_gitignore_entries(repo_root, missing)
... [120 lines omitted] ...
634→ # Non-fatal: warn but continue init 635→ warn_msg = f"Artifact sync failed: {sync_result.message}" 649→ warn_msg = f"{cap.name} install failed: {result.message}"
session.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. The user wants required capabilities to be invisible to the user. Currently:
1. `list_capabilities()` returns ALL capabilities, and it's used in: - `list_cmd.py` - the `erk init capability list` command - `add_cmd.py` and `remove_cmd.py` - for showing available capabilities when invalid name given - `main.py` line 682 - in Step 3 of `erk init` to show capability status - `sync.py` - for artifact sync
2. `list_required_capabilities()` returns required ones, used in `main.py` for auto-install.
The cleanest solution is to add a `visible` property to the `Capability` base class that defaults to `not self.required`, then make the 5 bundled skills required so they're automatically hidden from the UI, and update the list command to filter by visibility instead of showing everything.
Now I'm mapping out the specific files that need changes: I'll mark the bundled skills as required in `bundled.py`, filter the list view in `list_cmd.py`, hide required capabilities from the Step 3 output in `main.py`, and update the error messages in both `add_cmd.py` and `remove_cmd.py` to only show optional capabilities. I should also consider whether to prevent removing required capabilities entirely.
session.tool_result:
1→"""Add capabilities to a repository.""" 2→ 3→from pathlib import Path 4→ 5→import click 6→ 7→from erk.cli.commands.init.capability.backend_utils import resolve_backend 8→from erk.core.capabilities.registry import get_capability, list_capabilities 9→from erk.core.context import ErkContext 10→from erk.core.repo_discovery import NoRepoSentinel, discover_repo_or_sentinel 11→from erk_shared.output.output import user_output 12→ 13→ 14→@click.command("add") 15→@click.argument("names", nargs=-1, required=True) 16→@click.pass_obj 17→def add_cmd(ctx: ErkContext, names: tuple[str, ...]) -> None: 18→ """Install capabilities in the current repository or user settings. 19→ 20→ NAMES are the capability names to install. Multiple can be 21→ specified at once. 22→ 23→ Project-level capabilities require being in a git repository. 24→ User-level capabilities can be installed from anywhere. 25→ 26→ \b 27→ Examples: 28→ erk init capability add learned-docs 29→ erk init capability add learned-docs dignified-python 30→ erk init capability add statusline # user-level, works outside repos
... [71 lines omitted] ...
32→ # Track success/failure for exit code 33→ any_failed = False 47→ any_failed = True 67→ any_failed = True 87→ any_failed = True 97→ any_failed = True 99→ if any_failed:

1→"""Remove capabilities from a repository.""" 2→ 3→from pathlib import Path 4→ 5→import click 6→ 7→from erk.cli.commands.init.capability.backend_utils import resolve_backend 8→from erk.core.capabilities.registry import get_capability, list_capabilities 9→from erk.core.context import ErkContext 10→from erk.core.repo_discovery import NoRepoSentinel, discover_repo_or_sentinel 11→from erk_shared.output.output import user_output 12→ 13→ 14→@click.command("remove") 15→@click.argument("names", nargs=-1, required=True) 16→@click.pass_obj 17→def remove_cmd(ctx: ErkContext, names: tuple[str, ...]) -> None: 18→ """Remove capabilities from the current repository or user settings. 19→ 20→ NAMES are the capability names to remove. Multiple can be 21→ specified at once. 22→ 23→ Required capabilities (like erk-hooks) cannot be removed. 24→ 25→ \b 26→ Examples: 27→ erk init capability remove dignified-python 28→ erk init capability remove learned-docs devrun-agent 29→ erk init capability remove statusline # user-level 30→ """
... [66 lines omitted] ...
31→ # Track success/failure for exit code 32→ any_failed = False 46→ any_failed = True 54→ any_failed = True 74→ any_failed = True 92→ any_failed = True 94→ if any_failed:
session.assistant: Good. Now I have the full picture. The `remove_cmd` already blocks removing required capabilities. Now I need to update the plan to hide required capabilities from all user-facing lists.

tool_use.param:
# Make additional capabilities required and hide them from user-facing lists

tool_use.name: ExitPlanMode
tool_use.param:

session.tool_result:
PreToolUse:ExitPlanMode hook error: [command -v erk >/dev/null 2>&1 || exit 0; ERK_HOOK_ID=exit-plan-mode-hook erk exec exit-plan-mode-hook]: DISPLAY PLAN: Before asking the question below, read the plan file and display its contents to the user with proper markdown formatting: /Users/schrockn/.claude/plans/delegated-weaving-babbage.md
PLAN SAVE PROMPT
A plan exists for this session but has not been saved.
Use AskUserQuestion to ask the user: question: "📋 Make additional capabilities required and hide them from user-facing lists Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)\n\nWhat would you like to do with this plan?" header: "br:master"
IMPORTANT: Present options in this exact order: 1. "Create a plan PR" (Recommended) - Create a draft PR with the plan and stop. Does NOT proceed to implementation. 2. "Skip PR and implement here" - Skip creating a PR, implement directly in current worktree (for small PR iterations that don't need PR tracking). 3. "View/Edit the plan" - Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.
⚠️ WARNING: Currently on 'master'. We strongly discourage editing directly on the trunk branch. Consider saving the plan and implementing in a dedicated worktree instead.
If user chooses 'Create a plan PR': 1. Run /erk:plan-save 2. STOP - Do NOT call ExitPlanMode. The plan-save command handles everything. Stay in plan mode and let the user exit manually if desired.
If user chooses 'Skip PR and implement here': 1. Create implement-now marker (skip PR creation): erk exec marker create --session-id dde102f8-f26b-464a-a801-edb171c55897 \ exit-plan-mode-hook.implement-now 2. Call ExitPlanMode 3. After exiting plan mode, implement the changes directly
... [7 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
Here's the plan:
## Make additional capabilities required and hide them from user-facing lists
### Context Required capabilities should be invisible to users — they're system infrastructure, not optional enhancements. 5 bundled skills need to become required, and all required capabilities need to be hidden from user-facing lists.
### Changes (6 files)
1. **`src/erk/capabilities/skills/bundled.py`** — Add `_REQUIRED_BUNDLED_SKILLS` frozenset (`erk-diff-analysis`, `erk-exec`, `objective`, `pr-operations`, `pr-feedback-classifier`) and override `required` property on `BundledSkillCapability`
2. **`src/erk/core/capabilities/registry.py`** — Add `list_optional_capabilities()` that filters out `required=True` caps
3. **`src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/list_cmd.py`** — Use `list_optional_capabilities()` in list display and error hints
4. **`src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/add_cmd.py`** — Use `list_optional_capabilities()` in error hints
5. **`src/erk/cli/commands/init/capability/remove_cmd.py`** — Use `list_optional_capabilities()` in error hints
6. **`src/erk/cli/commands/init/main.py`** — Use `list_optional_capabilities()` in Step 3 display
Internal functions (`list_capabilities()`, `list_required_capabilities()`) unchanged.

tool_use.name: AskUserQuestion
tool_use.param: [{'question': '📋 Make additional capabilities required and hide them from user-facing list...[truncated 458 chars]...ion': 'Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

session.tool_result:
User has answered your questions: "📋 Make additional capabilities required and hide them from user-facing lists Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)
What would you like to do with this plan?"="Create a plan PR (Recommended)". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.
Launching skill: erk:plan-save
session.user:
# /erk:plan-save
Save the current session's plan to GitHub with session context.
## Usage
```bash /erk:plan-save # Standalone plan /erk:plan-save --plan-type=learn # Learn plan (erk-learn label) /erk:plan-save --objective=123 # Explicit objective link (overrides marker) ```
Objective linking: if a plan was created via `/erk:objective-plan`, the session's objective-context marker is read automatically by the save command. Use `--objective=<number>` to override the marker or provide the link when no marker exists (e.g., during replan).
## Plan Storage
The plan is saved using the configured backend:
- **Draft PR backend** (`PLAN_BACKEND = "draft_pr"`): Creates a branch, pushes a plan commit, and opens a draft PR. Plan content is in the PR body after the metadata separator. The JSON output contract includes `plan_number`, `plan_url`, `title`, `branch_name`, `plan_backend`.
## Agent Instructions
### Step 1: Parse Arguments
Check `` for the `--plan-type` and `--objective` flags:
``` If contains "--plan-type=<type>": - Extract the type (standard or learn) - Store as PLAN_TYPE variable - Set PLAN_TYPE_FLAG to "--plan-type=<type>" Else: - Set PLAN_TYPE_FLAG to empty string
If contains "--objective=<number>": - Extract the number - Set OBJECTIVE_FLAG to "--objective=<number>" Else: - Set OBJECTIVE_FLAG to empty string ```
### Step 2: Generate Branch Slug
Before saving, generate a branch slug from the plan title. Read the plan title from the plan file you wrote in plan mode (the first `# ` heading).
Generate a branch slug from the title:
- 2-4 hyphenated lowercase words, max 30 characters - Capture distinctive essence, drop filler words (the, a, for, implementation, plan) - Prefer action verbs: add, fix, refactor, update, consolidate, extract, migrate - Examples: "fix-auth-session", "add-plan-validation", "refactor-gateway-abc"
Store the result as `BRANCH_SLUG`.
### Step 3: Generate Plan Summary
Write a concise 2-3 sentence summary of the plan. This summary will be visible at the top of the PR description (above the collapsed full plan).
Guidelines:
- Focus on WHAT the plan does and WHY - Do not repeat the title - Plain text, no markdown headers or formatting - Avoid special shell characters (backticks, dollar signs) - Store as PLAN_SUMMARY
### Step 4: Run Save Command
Run this command with the session ID, branch slug, summary, and optional flags:
```bash erk exec plan-save --format json --session-id="dde102f8-f26b-464a-a801-edb171c55897" --branch-slug="${BRANCH_SLUG}" --summary="${PLAN_SUMMARY}" ${PLAN_TYPE_FLAG} ${OBJECTIVE_FLAG} ```
Parse the JSON output to extract `plan_number` for verification in Step 5.
If the command fails, display the error and stop.
### Step 5: Verify Objective Link (if applicable)
**Only run this step if `objective_issue` is non-null in the JSON output from Step 4.**
Verify the objective link was saved correctly:
```bash erk exec get-plan-metadata <plan_number> objective_issue ```
Parse the JSON response:
- If `success: true` and `value` matches the expected objective number: verification passed - If `success: false` or value doesn't match: verification failed
**On verification success:**
Display: `Verified objective link: #<objective-number>`
**On verification failure:**
Display error and remediation steps:
``` ERROR: Objective link verification failed Expected objective: #<expected> Actual: <actual-or-null>
The plan was saved but without the correct objective link. Fix: Close draft PR #<plan_number>, ensure the objective-context marker exists, and re-run /erk:plan-save. ```
Exit without creating the plan-saved marker. The session continues so the user can retry.
### Step 6: Update Objective Roadmap (if objective linked)
**Only run this step if `objective_issue` was non-null in JSON output and verification passed.**
Update the objective's roadmap table to show that a plan has been created for this node:
1. **Read the roadmap node marker** to get the node ID:
```bash step_id=$(erk exec marker read --session-id "dde102f8-f26b-464a-a801-edb171c55897" roadmap-step) ```
If the marker doesn't exist (command fails), skip this step - the plan wasn't created via `objective-plan`.
2. **Update the roadmap table** using the dedicated command:
```bash erk exec update-objective-node <objective-issue> --node "$step_id" --pr "#<plan_number>" --status in_progress ```
This atomically fetches the objective body, finds the matching node row, sets the Status cell to `in-progress`, and writes the updated body back.
3. **Report the update:**
Display: `Updated objective #<objective-issue> roadmap: node <step_id> → plan #<plan_number>`
**Error handling:** If the roadmap update fails, warn but continue - the plan was saved successfully, just the roadmap tracking didn't update. The user can manually update the objective.
### Step 7: Display Results
**If JSON contains `skipped_duplicate: true`:**
Display: `Plan already saved as #<plan_number> (duplicate skipped)`
If `branch_name` is present in the JSON, display the same next-steps block as the success case below.
If `branch_name` is absent, display only: `View PR: <plan_url>`
Return immediately (skip Steps 5, 6 above if not already executed).
**Otherwise, on success**, display:
``` Plan "<title>" saved as draft PR #<plan_number> URL: <issue_url> ```
**Slot options block:**
The "OR exit Claude Code first" section should show both slot allocation options, with the recommended one listed first based on trunk detection:
If **on trunk = true**:
``` OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
New slot (recommended — you're on trunk): Local: erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Same slot: Local: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If **on trunk = false**:
``` OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
Same slot (recommended — you're in a slot): Local: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
New slot: Local: erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
**Next steps block:**
``` Next steps:
View PR: <plan_url>
In Claude Code: Dispatch to queue: /erk:pr-dispatch — Dispatch plan for remote agent implementation
OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of: Checkout: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If objective was verified, also display: `Verified objective link: #<objective-number>`
If the JSON output contains `slot_name` and `slot_objective_updated: true`, also display: `Slot objective updated: <slot_name> → #<objective-number>`
**Note:** Slot objective updates are handled automatically by `plan-save` when an objective is linked via the session marker - no separate command call needed.
On failure, display the error message and suggest:
- Checking that a plan exists (enter Plan mode and exit it first) - Verifying GitHub CLI authentication (`gh auth status`) - Checking network connectivity
### Step 5: Push Planning Session
After saving successfully, push the planning session for cross-machine learning. This captures the in-progress session state so it's available even if implementation happens on a different machine.
Run `erk exec capture-session-info` to get the session file path, then push it:
```bash erk exec upload-impl-session --session-id "dde102f8-f26b-464a-a801-edb171c55897" 2>/dev/null || true ```
If the plan was saved successfully and `plan_number` is known, use push-session directly for better stage tracking:
```bash erk exec push-session \ --session-file "<session_file_path>" \ --session-id "dde102f8-f26b-464a-a801-edb171c55897" \ --stage planning \ --source local \ --plan-id <plan_number> \ 2>/dev/null || true ```
Where `<session_file_path>` is obtained from `erk exec capture-session-info` output and `<plan_number>` is from Step 2's JSON output.
**Note:** This is non-critical. If it fails, the plan was still saved successfully. The `2>/dev/null || true` ensures graceful degradation.
## Session Tracking
After successfully saving a plan, the plan number is stored in a marker file that enables automatic plan updates in the same session.
**To read the saved plan number:**
```bash erk exec marker read --session-id "dde102f8-f26b-464a-a801-edb171c55897" plan-saved-issue ```
This returns the plan number (exit code 0) or exits with code 1 if no plan was saved in this session.
session.thinking:
Let me follow the plan-save instructions. I need to:
1. Parse arguments - no special flags 2. Generate a branch slug from the plan title 3. Generate a plan summary 4. Run the save command
The plan title is "Make additional capabilities required and hide them from user-facing lists"
Branch slug: "hide-required-capabilities" (concise, action verb, under 30 chars)
Plan summary: Makes 5 bundled skills (erk-diff-analysis, erk-exec, objective, pr-operations, pr-feedback-classifier) required so they auto-install during erk init. Adds list_optional_capabilities() to the registry and updates all user-facing CLI commands to hide required capabilities from display.

**Plan summary:** Makes 5 bundled skills required so they auto-install during erk init. Adds list_optional_capabilities to the registry and updates all user-facing CLI commands to hide required capabilities from display.
